title: Talking about my life - Boulder, Colorado 14/21

transcript.text: I remember thinking, how the hell did you, did you fold your, how the hell did you keep all of your homework that impeccable?
Like there aren't even ruffles on the side of your, on the corners of your pages.
Like, how did you fold that so perfectly?
Like, do you practice that?
Like, and, and the handwriting is like, some of the handwriting was really like crispy and I was like, damn.
Um, but I didn't even have any desire to try to be like that.
I was just like, whatever.
Um, but it was boring.
I didn't, they didn't ask.
They didn't, what I really, really, really, really wished that they would do.
And maybe I should have voiced, I should have voiced my opinions more, but my parents were like, really like freaked out about me.
Like my parents, my parents didn't like me.
My parents saw, I mean, they loved me and everything, but they saw me, especially in that, in that time area.
They saw me as a threat.
They saw me as a obstacle.
They saw me as something that they needed to fight.
Um, so if I ever said anything like about expressing my opinions, they would have just, they would have just attacked me and told me to like be normal.
You know, like, like, um, like if I would have said, oh, they're not, they're not, I wish, I wish we had a class where we could write.
I wish we had a class where we could just write.
I want to write, you know, I want to express my creativity and then, you know, and get to read my story to the class.
You know, my parents would have been like, well, then write.
And I'd be like, no, I wish there was a class where you could write like school credit for it, you know?
And then, I don't know, they were just always like getting angry with me if I ever had like an issue.
Like I, I had to, I had to obey and like be and agree with everything that's just the way the system was and agree with it.
And if I didn't, then that was like, I was like a mentally ill bad kid.
And I, and so, yeah, they, they had me trying to get me on pills.
They try to get me on all these different pills, man.
Every year it was like a different, it was a different drug because my mom was a psychiatric nurse.
And so she dealt with that kind of stuff and it was really into it.
Tried to get me on, well, I mean, right now my mom's always trying to get me on Dexedrine.
And then she tried to get me on lithium when I was in college.
And then there was these other drugs, Ritalin, um, and then some other one, Prozac.
And then having, sending me off to these like, uh, psychologists, child psychologists.
My mom made me go to a psychologist just like three years ago.
And the guy's like, I remember you, man.
I remember seeing you when you were a little kid, dude.
And I was like, oh God.
But yeah, they had me, they had me send, and they were like religious.
They're like Buddhists.
So they're always like dabbling all these like new kinds of like,
My dad was into NLP for a while, went off to the NLP conferences, like in Steamboat and stuff.
And so they get these like weird psychologists to like be weird.
Like some guy being like, so tell me about it, something.
And I'd be like, uh, and he'd be like, tell me more.
And he, this guy just wasn't really good.
And then some other guy, some other guy was nice, but they had me to all these different people.
Like there was something wrong with me, which pissed me off.
And so then I'd fight them and then they'd, I'd make them cry a couple of times.
But, uh, but basically, basically I had the, I lived in a household where I was not supposed to rebel at all.
And if I did, it was a war between both of my parents.
So basically I just had to buck up and, and just do what everybody told me to do.
But in retrospect, what I should have done is just refused to go to school.
I, cause there's nothing they could have done.
They couldn't have kicked me out of the house.
They couldn't have sent me, they couldn't have forced me to go to school.
Maybe they would have sent me off to some like juvenile detention center area, but that would have been,
Or like some kind of boarding school or something.
But, you know, maybe that would have been better because that there would have been more like opportunity to have like some kind of an alternative,
Like encourage me to like express, you know, write, write my own thing, create my own thing.
But yeah, school wasn't all that fun, dude.
And then we had this other dude named, uh, Mr. Tidwell.
No, that was Tidwell.
Seemed like my, all of our teachers were like hillbillies.
Tidwell was like some kind of a hillbilly dude.
Really old too.
They're really old teachers.
They're all probably dead by now.
Mrs. Robinson was like 70.
And then she only taught us there at seven, like, in like the first semester of the first, or maybe the first year.
Because I guess she got a job at McDonald's because all the kids were like, we saw her at McDonald's.
And I went into a McDonald's once and I think I saw her there actually working at a McDonald's.
I think maybe she got fired or something.
But then we had a science teacher named Mr., um, oh my God, I can't remember.
Mr. Whistler?
There was a Mr. Whistler.
I don't know if I ever had him.
He was this like really old guy who was like really ugly.
Um, but I don't think I ever had him.
But there was this other guy named Mr. Something.
Who was like this really old guy with this really long beard.
And, and he'd go on these like speeches, these like diatribes that had nothing to do with the science.
Although science classes I enjoyed.
I liked science class.
Um, but he would go off on the stories like about, he was like some kind of like, he's like, yeah, if anybody tries to break in my house, I'll kill him, man.
Um, you know, I talk, I call my friends bastard.
I go, hey, you bastard.
Cause we're friends.
But if anybody who's not my friend calls me a bastard, then I'll fucking kill him.
You know, he was like, he was weird, man.
But, um, but I remember it was in seventh grade and he's like, who raise your hand in this class.
If you're over a hundred pounds and I didn't raise my hand.
So I was less than a hundred pounds in seventh grade.
I think it was less than five feet tall too.
I was a little guy and I hit puberty late too.
I didn't have hair on my dick until in eighth grade and seventh grade.
I didn't cause I remember looking at other kids and they had hair, but I didn't, I got it in like eighth grade or something.
So I guess I was like a late bloomer.
One kid, Nathaniel Daw, or was it Nathaniel Daw?
Cause yeah, he was in seventh grade.
He was really hairy in seventh grade and there was this ninth grader who was like really fucking hairy.
But, um, but yeah, I didn't, all the girls were taller than me.
I think in eighth grade too.
Wasn't, I don't think it was until ninth grade where I started being like taller than most of the girls.
And, but there were still some girls who were taller than me, but I was, yeah, I was little, I was a little guy.
I was like the shortest kid, shortest boy in the whole school.
There were girls shorter than me, but there was like, yeah, maybe I was the shortest kid.
I don't remember any kids who were shorter than me in seventh grade.
Um, but I got taller like towards the end of the year in seventh grade.
Cause I did like, I did this, I, there was this Spanish teacher named Mr. Roper.
And, um, I wasn't really good at languages either.
Cause they taught it, they taught it really like, they taught it incrementally.
They'd be like this week, we're going to do this.
So they're like, yeah, and then this and this, and I would be like, and so then I would get, lose my interest.
I couldn't focus if they're going to teach it so slowly.
So that by the time they got to the next lesson, I couldn't, I didn't have the first one.
And I mean, if you want to teach me right, you need to teach me all of it all at once.
Teach me first, past, present, future.
Teach me it all at once.
And expect me to know it all right away.
And if I don't, then it's kind of like up to me to figure it all out.
And then you just kind of, I mean, you explain it all one at a time, but you do it really quickly.
And then you review it.
And then you review it again, instead of like do the entire three, first three weeks on the first present.
Because I'm going to get hazy and like fall asleep.
But anyways, he was like the wrestling coach.
And I remember him going like, no, but it's wrestling.
It's real wrestling.
It's not Hulk Hogan wrestling.
So Hulk Hogan was a star in 87.
So I joined the wrestling team and I wrestled some kid named Sean Fleming.
And we like tied, even though he was a lot taller than me.
But then I wrestled Stuart Robertson and he like threw me down.
Like immediately just smacked me down.
And I saw him like in class the next day or like in the hallway.
And he was with a bunch of kids and he's like, hey you, how come you didn't show up for wrestling?
How come you dropped out?
And he was like way taller than me, like way bigger than me.
But by the end of the year, I was as big as him.
So I kind of hit my growth spurt like in seventh grade.
But yeah, who was another teacher we had?
We had this one dude named Mr. Reno and maybe we didn't have him until seventh grade or until eighth grade.
But he was a dick.
I did not like him at all and nobody liked him.
He was just an asshole.
He was really angry and all the time and just getting pissed off.
And he's like got pissed off at me too.
Like, which is stupid.
You don't want to get pissed off at me because I'm like the kid who's like the most,
I'm like the most, you know, obedient.
I try to, you know, I never interrupt.
I never show up late.
I always turn the homework and assignment on time.
But this, but you know, sometimes I don't pay attention.
And I think sometimes they thought that because I wasn't paying attention because I was just like bored that I was like doing that on purpose.
Because like one time he's like, I was writing on a pen and you weren't supposed to write with a pen.
Like we were supposed to like be paying attention to him or something.
He's like, yeah, you don't want to, you shouldn't be writing with a pen like this guy.
And he points to me and I was like, I thought that was overdoing it.
But we, but in ninth grade, we had the ninth grade off campus comedian.
He was like the teacher sponsor and he'd proofread all of like our proposals.
We'd have to write to the school board and to the different, um, merchants around there who were supposed to support us.
And he was being, he was really cool then.
He was really friendly to us then.
And, uh, proofreading it.
I think I was the only guy on that off campus committee.
It was like Brie Minger was the president.
I was a secretary.
So I was the guy who like typed it all up.
And then like Gina Davis was something else.
She was another girl.
Those are the only people I remember, but it was like five or six people on it.
Gina Davis wanted to be like an astronaut.
She was the sister of what's that other girl, Davis, Susan butcher.
No, no.
Gina butcher was a sister of Susan butcher who won the Adidas rod.
She wanted to be an astronaut.
And I asked her, I was like, do you want to be an astronaut?
Because you like flying or because you like science?
She was like both.
Cool.
That was interesting.
She's the only kid who I knew who wanted to do, knew what she wanted to do.
Although there was this other kid named Brian.
Something or other.
There was this like blonde kid who hung out with, who only hung out with girls.
And I think he was gay.
Because he would, he was like really emotional.
He'd like cry and stuff.
But he'd only have it with gay, with girls.
And like a lot of people would make fun of him all the time.
And he was a, he was a, he was a, he was a figure skater.
A competitive figure skater.
Yeah.
I think he was like pretty good.
Like he'd go to all the competitions and stuff.
But, uh, man, kids were cruel back then.
Some kids are so fucking cruel.
They'd be like, you're a figure skater.
Ha ha.
I wasn't, I was never the kid who got picked on though.
I wasn't one of the kids who got picked on.
I was definitely one of the kids in grade school who certain kids decided they hated,
They hated.
But that was different from being somebody who gets picked on.
Somebody who gets picked on is somebody who like more than one person doesn't like them.
And I don't think I had never had like more than one person, not like me, but this other
Kid who they picked on with a lot was Royal Smith.
And I felt really bad for him because he was this fat kid.
And for some reason he didn't shower or at least he smelled like he didn't shower.
Cause he had this like smell and his like sliminess all over him.
And when kids would like grab him, like touch him, like playing tag football or whatever,
They'd be like, we got slimed by Royal.
And he'd get like really upset.
Like one time he got really upset and banging his head against the wall, against the door.
And, but he was my friend because we, we had choir together and we sat next to each
Other in choir.
And I think we were even like helper bees together in like a girl's choir for a little while
As if you need to help her bees.
It was like, it was like covering a requirement.
That's another thing that's fucked up about school.
You do classes you don't need to take just for some requirement.
I mean, fuck requirements.
It's like, yeah, I remember once Ken Lawless slammed him up against the, cause he was kind
Of a short kid.
He wasn't like physically big, slammed him up against the lockers.
And then, and then in class and choir, he comes up to me and he's like, I can't believe
You did that, Kyle.
I was like, what?
I can't believe you let Ken slam me up against the locker.
You just walked away.
And I was like, Oh, and I go, well, you know, I don't like to fight.
I'd rather run away than fight.
But in retrospect, that was kind of bad.
I shouldn't, I shouldn't have done that.
But, um, but, uh, yeah.
In seventh grade, who did I hang out with?
Do at the very beginning.
And then Chris Jenkins, who was Moses Jenkins before that, he was actually my neighbor with
Like a block up from me who I hung out with.
And he had these two older sisters or like three older sisters.
And, uh, I was, I think he went to like June, uh, uni Hill, but I mainly hung out with him
Cause he was a neighbor.
And then it's, yeah, I hung out with him for a little bit cause he was friends with this
Ethiopian kid and we hung out with him for a little bit.
I didn't really hang out with the same people for more than like a little, like a few weeks
And then I'd hang out and then I hang out by myself again.
And then, but like we, we ditched school with him once and the Ethiopian guy.
After lunch, we just left school and like roamed around the, uh, the, uh, um, woo, out
Of time.
